Prior UTI episode 18 months ago increases risk of recurrence. Inadequate fluid intake is also noted as a contributing factor.
A previous UTI episode approximately 18 months ago is explicitly documented, treated with antibiotics and resolved without complications.
Mother has a documented history of recurrent urinary tract infections (N39.0), representing direct family history of the same disease.
Father has type 2 diabetes mellitus, a known related disorder that predisposes to recurrent UTIs.
Prior UTI history, inadequate daily fluid intake, and prolonged sitting at work (urinary stasis risk) are recognized risk factors for UTI in males.

The patient has no active comorbidities (e.g., diabetes, immunosuppression, structural urinary anomaly). His father has diabetes but the patient himself has normal fasting glucose, so no predisposing comorbidity is present.
Inadequate fluid intake on workdays is explicitly noted, contributing to urinary stasis, which is a recognized behavioral risk factor for UTI.
Prolonged sedentary desk work is associated with urinary stasis, a known risk factor for UTI, and is relevant to this patient's occupation as a software developer.
Recreational cycling and football are not established triggers or risk factors for UTI in males; no hobby-related risk is evident.
The patient takes no regular prescription medications prior to this episode. None of the listed medications (ciprofloxacin, nitrofurantoin, ibuprofen) are known to cause UTI.
A 5-day history of progressive dysuria, frequency, and nocturia without fever >38°C is temporally consistent with an uncomplicated lower UTI, which typically develops over days.
Ciprofloxacin and nitrofurantoin are both standard first-line treatments for UTI (N39.0), so not all medications are unrelated to the primary diagnosis.
